whatItDoes: Generates bcrypt password hashes and verifies passwords against existing bcrypt hashes. Supports configurable cost rounds (4-14), real-time hash generation, and one-click copy to clipboard.
whyItExists: Bcrypt is the industry standard for password hashing, but developers often need a quick way to generate hashes for testing or verify passwords without setting up a full backend. This tool provides a browser-based interface for both operations.
whoShouldUse: Backend developers implementing authentication systems, security engineers testing password storage, and developers migrating or verifying user credentials.
useCases: Generate a bcrypt hash of a password for storage in a database
Verify that a plaintext password matches an existing bcrypt hash
Test different cost rounds to balance security and performance
Quickly produce bcrypt hashes for seed data or test fixtures
Debug authentication issues by verifying password-hash pairs
instructions: Select the mode: 'Generate' to create a new hash or 'Verify' to check a password against a hash
In Generate mode, enter your password and select the desired cost rounds (default: 10)
The bcrypt hash appears below with the standard $2b$ prefix
In Verify mode, enter the password and paste the bcrypt hash to check for a match
Click 'Copy' to copy the generated hash to your clipboard
examples: Verify: password 'hello' against hash '$2b$10$...' → Result: Match / No Match
bestPractices: Use cost rounds of at least 10 for production password hashing
Never store plaintext passwords; always use bcrypt or a similar adaptive hash
Bcrypt hashes are 60 characters long and self-contain the cost and salt
Higher cost rounds increase security but also increase computation time
commonMistakes: Using a cost factor that is too low (below 8) making hashes easy to brute-force
Confusing bcrypt with plain SHA-256 hashing, which is not suitable for passwords
Truncating the bcrypt hash, which invalidates the entire hash
Using different prefixes ($2a$ vs $2b$) when comparing hashes from different libraries
faq: What is the cost rounds parameter? It determines how many times the hashing algorithm iterates, exponentially increasing computation time (2^cost iterations).
Why does the output always look different for the same password? Bcrypt includes a random salt, so each hash is unique even for identical inputs.
Is bcrypt still secure? Yes, bcrypt is an adaptive hash function designed to remain secure as computing power increases.
What is the difference between $2a$, $2b$, and $2y$? They are minor format variations from different implementations; $2b$ is the current standard.
